Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); read2(x); write2(x); write1(x); write1(y).

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); write1(y); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); commit1(x,y).

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Teaches.advisor = Scientist.id AND Teaches.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Teaches.student = Scientist.id AND Teaches.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T2.advisor, T1.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.advisor = T2.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (SELECT T.advisor AS advisor, T.student AS student FROM Teaches T UNION SELECT T.advisor AS advisor, A.student AS student FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T WHERE A.advisor = T.student) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants(id, name, university, year) AS (SELECT S.id AS id, S.name AS name, S.university AS university, S.year AS year FROM Teaches T, Scientist S WHERE T.student = S.id AND T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus') UNION SELECT S.id AS id, S.name AS name, S.university AS university, S.year AS year FROM Descendants D, Teaches T, Scientist S WHERE D.id = T.advisor AND T.student = S.id) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) FROM Scientist S LEFT JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.name ORDER BY COUNT(T.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.advisor WHERE T.student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T on S.id = T.student WHERE T.advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor AS grandparent, T2.student as grandchild FROM Teaches T1  JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE IDK AS (   SELECT DISTINCT * FROM Teaches T   UNION ALL   SELECT T.advisor, T.student FROM Teaches T, IDK R WHERE R.student = T.advisor   )   SELECT * FROM IDK;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ErasmusLegacy AS (   SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S JOIN Teaches T ON S.id = T.student WHERE T.Advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')   UNION   SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM ErasmusLegacy E, Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE E.id = T.advisor AND S.id = T.student )   SELECT * FROM ErasmusLegacy;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x), read1(y), write1(x), write1(y), read2(x), commit1(x,y), write2(x), commit2(x)

Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) AS num_students FROM Scientist s LEFT JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.name ORDER BY num_students DESC, s.name;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor JOIN Scientist e ON t.student = e.id WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Scientist e ON t.advisor = e.id WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT grandparent.advisor, child.student FROM Teaches AS child JOIN Teaches AS parent ON child.advisor = parent.student JOIN Teaches AS grandparent ON parent.advisor = grandparent.student WHERE grandparent.advisor <> child.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT t.advisor, a.student     FROM Teaches t     JOIN Ancestors a ON t.student = a.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student     JOIN Scientist e ON t.advisor = e.id     WHERE e.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION ALL       SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year     FROM Scientist s     JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student     JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x);  commit1(x,y)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY count(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE advisor = id AND student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE student = id AND advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT Grandchild.advisor, Grandparent.student FROM Teaches Grandparent, Teaches Grandchild WHERE Grandparent.student = Grandchild.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE StudentAncestor(id) AS ( SELECT T.advisor AS id FROM Teaches T UNION SELECT T.advisor AS id FROM StudentAncestor S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.student AND NOT T.advisor IS NULL) SELECT * FROM StudentAncestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AncestorOfErasmus(id) AS (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT Teaches.student FROM Teaches, AncestorOfErasmus WHERE Teaches.advisor = AncestorOfErasmus.id) SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, AncestorOfErasmus WHERE Scientist.id = AncestorOfErasmus.id;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE DesideriusTheAncestor AS (     SELECT S2.id, S2.name, S2.university, S2.year     FROM Scientist S1, Scientist S2, Teaches T     WHERE S1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     AND T.advisor = S1.id     AND T.student = S2.id     UNION     SELECT S3.id, S3.name, S3.university, S3.year     FROM DesideriusTheAncestor A, Scientist S3, Teaches T     WHERE T.advisor = A.id     AND T.student = S3.id ) SELECT * FROM DesideriusTheAncestor;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT *  FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(Teaches.student) FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.id, Scientist.name ORDER BY COUNT(Teaches.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor JOIN Scientist erasmus ON erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Teaches.student = erasmus.id;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.student JOIN Scientist erasmus ON erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Teaches.advisor = erasmus.id;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Advisor_names(id, name, university, year) AS (     SELECT id, name, university, year     FROM Scientist     WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION ALL     SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year     FROM Scientist     JOIN Teaches ON Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor     JOIN Advisor_names a ON Teaches.student = a.id ) SELECT * FROM Advisor_names;

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE HierarAdv AS ( SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION ALL SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches t1 JOIN HierarAdv t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM HierarAdv;

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE HierarAdv AS (     SELECT advisor, student     FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student     FROM Teaches t1     JOIN HierarAdv t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM HierarAdv;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE DesEra AS ( SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION ALL SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN DesEra d ON t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM DesEra;

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Scientist.name, COUNT(*) AS amount FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor GROUP BY Scientist.name ORDER BY amount DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS ( SELECT Teaches.advisor, Teaches.student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT A1.advisor, A2.student FROM Ancestors A1, Ancestors A2, Teaches WHERE Teaches.advisor = A1.student AND Teaches.student = A2.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Grandparent AS( SELECT Teaches.* FROM Teaches UNION SELECT Teaches.advisor, Grandparent.student  FROM Teaches, Grandparent WHERE Grandparent.advisor = Teaches.student ) SELECT * FROM Grandparent;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS ( SELECT Scientist.* FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist S2 WHERE S2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Scientist.id = Teaches.student AND S2.id = Teaches.advisor UNION SELECT Scientist.* FROM Scientist, Teaches, Descendants  WHERE Descendants.id= Teaches.advisor AND Scientist.id = Teaches.student  ) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student WHERE s.university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s WHERE s.university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(t.student) FROM Scientist s, Teaches t WHERE s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.id, s.name ORDER BY COUNT(t.student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s, teaches t, Scientist student WHERE s.id = t.advisor AND t.student = student.id AND student.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT student.id, student.name, student.university, student.year FROM Scientist s, teaches t, Scientist student WHERE s.id = t.advisor AND t.student = student.id AND s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT grandparent.id, grandchild.id FROM Scientist grandparent, Scientist father, Scientist grandchild, Teaches t, Teaches t2 WHERE grandparent.id = t.advisor AND t.student = father.id AND father.id = t2.advisor AND t2.student = grandchild.id;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (SELECT advisor, student, advisor AS root FROM Teaches UNION SELECT t.advisor, t.student, a.root FROM Teaches t JOIN Ancestors a ON t.advisor = a.student) SELECT DISTINCT root AS advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (SELECT id, name, university, year, id AS root FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year, d.root FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id) SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Descendants WHERE NOT name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT S2.id, S2.name, S2.university, S2.year FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist as S2 WHERE Scientist.id = Teaches.advisor AND Teaches.student = S2.id AND Scientist.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches as T1, Teaches as T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor, student) AS (       SELECT advisor, student       FROM Teaches       UNION       SELECT T1.advisor, A.student       FROM ancestors A, teaches T1       WHERE A.advisor = T1.Student ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants(id, name, university, year) AS (       SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year       FROM Scientist S, Teaches T, Scientist S2       WHERE T.student = S.id       AND T.advisor = S2.id       AND S2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'       UNION       SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year       FROM Descendants D, Scientist S, Teaches T       WHERE D.id = T.advisor       AND T.student = S.id ) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write1(y); write2(x)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write1(y); commit1(x,y); write2(x); commit2(x)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT  *  FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist AS S, Teaches AS T WHERE S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist AS S WHERE id IN (     SELECT advisor     FROM Teaches AS T     WHERE T.student = (         SELECT id         FROM Scientist AS S         WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     ) );

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT *  FROM Scientist AS S WHERE S.id IN ( SELECT T.student  FROM Teaches AS T WHERE T.advisor= (SELECT id FROM Scientist AS S WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus') );

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(advisor,student) AS (   SELECT T.advisor, T.student    FROM Teaches AS T   UNION   SELECT T.advisor, T.student    FROM Ancestors AS A, Teaches AS T   WHERE T.advisor = A.student) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year  FROM Scientist AS S WHERE S.id IN ( SELECT student FROM Teaches AS T WHERE T.advisor = ( SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' ) );

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor (advisor, student) AS  (SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT T.advisor, A.student FROM Teaches T, Ancestor A WHERE T.student = A.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants (id, name, university, year) AS  (SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Scientist S, Teaches T, Descendants D WHERE S.id = T.student AND D.id = T.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); read2(x); write2(x); write1(x); write1(y);

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y); commit1(x,y)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(name) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(name) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = advisor AND student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = student AND advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AncestorsOfStudent(advisor, student) AS ( SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM AncestorsOfStudent T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor  ) SELECT * FROM AncestorsOfStudent;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE DescendentsOf(student) AS ( SELECT student FROM Teaches WHERE advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus') UNION SELECT T2.student FROM DescendentsOf T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student = T2.advisor  ) SELECT id, name, university, year FROM DescendentsOf, Scientist WHERE id = student;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor JOIN Scientist student ON t.student = student.id WHERE student.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Scientist advisor ON t.advisor = advisor.id WHERE advisor.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE GrandparentChild AS (   SELECT t1.advisor AS grandparent, t1.student   FROM Teaches t1     UNION ALL     SELECT t2.advisor, t2.student   FROM Teaches t2   JOIN GrandparentChild gc ON t2.advisor = gc.student )

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AdvisorDescendant AS (   SELECT advisor, student   FROM Teaches     UNION ALL     SELECT t1.advisor, t2.student   FROM AdvisorDescendant t1   JOIN Teaches t2 ON t1.student = t2.advisor )   SELECT * FROM AdvisorDescendant;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (   SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year   FROM Scientist s   WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'     UNION ALL     SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year   FROM Scientist s   JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor   JOIN Descendants d ON t.student = d.id )   SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor AS ( SELECT T.advisor, T.student FROM Teaches T UNION SELECT T.advisor, A.student FROM Teaches T, Ancestor A WHERE A.advisor = T.student ) SELECT * FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendant AS ( SELECT Sc.id, Sc.name, Sc.university, Sc.year FROM Scientist Sc WHERE Sc.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'  UNION SELECT Sc.id, Sc.name, Sc.university, Sc.year FROM Scientist Sc, Descendant D WHERE Sc.id = D.id ) SELECT * FROM Descendant;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read2(x), write1(x), write2(x), read1(x), write1(y), read1(y)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: write1(x), read1(y), read2(x), write2(x), commit2(x), read1(x), write1(y), commit1(x,y)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id=advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY student DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE id =ANY (SELECT advisor FROM Teaches WHERE student =ANY (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'));

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE Scientist.id =ANY (SELECT Teaches.student FROM Teaches WHERE advisor =ANY (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'));

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT P2.advisor, P1.student FROM Teaches P1, Teaches P2 WHERE P1.advisor = P2.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(ancestor) AS ( SELECT advisor AS ancestor FROM Teaches WHERE student IS NOT NULL UNION  SELECT advisor AS ancestor FROM Ancestors, Teaches WHERE Ancestors.ancestor= Teaches.advisorAND NOT advisor IS NULL )   SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(id, name, university, year) AS( SELECT DISTINCT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE Teaches.advisor = (SELECT DISTINCT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus') UNION SELECT DISTINCT advisor AS id, name, university, year FROM Ancestors, Teaches WHERE Ancestors.id = Teaches.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist WHERE university='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name,COUNT(student) AS count FROM Scientist,Teaches WHERE advisor=id GROUP BY name ORDER BY count DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s1.id,s1.name,s1.university,s1.year FROM Scientist AS s1,Teaches,Scientist AS s2 WHERE s1.id=advisor AND s2.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s2.id = student;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s2.id,s2.name,s2.university,s2.year FROM Scientist AS s1,Teaches,Scientist AS s2 WHERE s1.id=advisor AND s1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s2.id = student;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT t1.advisor,t1.student FROM Teaches AS t1,Scientist,Teaches AS t2 WHERE id = t1.advisor AND id = t2.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Find(advisor,student) AS ( SELECT advisor,student FROM Teaches  UNION SELECT T.advisor,F.student FROM Find F, Teaches T WHERE F.advisor = T.student ) SELECT * FROM Find;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Find(id,name,university,year) AS( SELECT s2.id,s2.name,s2.university,s2.year  FROM  Scientist AS s1,Teaches,Scientist AS s2  WHERE  s1.id=advisor AND s1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND s2.id = student   UNION SELECT T.student,S.name,S.university,S.year FROM Find F, Teaches T,Scientist S WHERE F.id = T.advisor AND S.id = T.student   ) SELECT * FROM Find;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors (advi_id, stud_id) AS(   SELECT S.id AS advi_id, T.student AS stud_id FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor   UNION   SELECT A.advi_id, T.student AS stud_id FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T, Scientist S WHERE A.stud_id = T.advisor AND T.student IS NOT NULL ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors (advi_id, stud_id) AS(   SELECT S.id AS advi_id, T.student AS stud_id FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND S.id = T.advisor   UNION   SELECT A.advi_id, T.student AS stud_id FROM Ancestors A, Teaches T, Scientist S WHERE A.stud_id = T.advisor AND T.student IS NOT NULL ) SELECT S.* FROM Ancestors, Scientist S WHERE Ancestors.stud_id = S.id;

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist WHERE university='Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name,COUNT(student) FROM Scientist,Teaches WHERE id=advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist,Teaches WHERE id=advisor AND student=(SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id,name,university,year FROM Scientist,Teaches WHERE id=student AND advisor=(SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT T.advisor,T1.student FROM Teaches T,Teaches T1 WHERE T.student=T1.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestor(a,s) AS  ( SELECT advisor AS a,student AS s FROM Teaches UNION Select Ancestor.a,Teaches.student FROM Ancestor,Teaches WHERE Ancestor.s=Teaches.advisor ) SELECT * FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendant(id1,name1,university1,year1) AS  ( SELECT id AS id1, name AS name1, university AS university1, year AS year1 FROM Scientist WHERE name='Desiderius Erasmus' UNION SELECT S.id,S.name,S.university,S.year FROM Descendant,Scientist S,Teaches WHERE S.id=Teaches.student AND Teaches.advisor=Descendant.id1 ) SELECT * FROM Descendant;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors(anc, des) AS ( SELECT Teaches.advisor, Teaches.student FROM Teaches WHERE Teaches.advisor IS NOT NULL   UNION   SELECT Teaches.advisor, Ancestors.des FROM Ancestors, Teaches WHERE Teaches.student = Ancestors.anc AND Teaches.advisor IS NOT NULL   ) SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT S.name, COUNT(T.student) AS st_num FROM Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE S.id = T.advisor GROUP BY S.name ORDER BY st_num DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.

Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ancestor AS (     SELECT *     FROM Teaches     UNION     SELECT T1.advisor, T2.student     FROM ancestor T1     JOIN Teaches T2 ON T1.student = T2.advisor ) SELECT *  FROM ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)

Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.
Answer Match: read1(x); read2(x); write2(x); read1(y); write1(x); write1(y);

Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); commit2(x); write1(y); commit1(x,y)

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist  WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist  WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student)  FROM Scientist JOIN Teaches ON (id = advisor) GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student)  DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist JOIN ( SELECT advisor FROM Teaches JOIN ( SELECT id  FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')  ON (student = id)) ON (id = advisor);

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist JOIN ( SELECT student FROM Teaches JOIN ( SELECT id  FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus')  ON (advisor = id)) ON (id = student);

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T2.advisor, T1.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.advisor = T2.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE AdvisorDescendant AS ( SELECT advisor, student, 1 AS level FROM Teaches  UNION SELECT T.advisor, AD.student, AD.level + 1 FROM Teaches T JOIN AdvisorDescendant AD ON T.student = AD.advisor ) SELECT DISTINCT advisor, student FROM AdvisorDescendant WHERE level > 1;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS ( SELECT id, name, university, year FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' -- Starting point: Erasmus UNION  SELECT S.id, S.name, S.university, S.year FROM Descendants D JOIN Teaches T ON D.id = T.advisor JOIN Scientist S ON T.student = S.id ) SELECT DISTINCT * FROM Descendants WHERE name != 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT s.name AS scientist_name, COUNT(t.student) AS num_students_supervised FROM Scientist s LEFT JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor GROUP BY s.id, s.name ORDER BY num_students_supervised DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.advisor JOIN Scientist erasmus ON t.student = erasmus.id WHERE erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student JOIN Scientist erasmus ON t.advisor = erasmus.id WHERE erasmus.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT gpa.advisor, gc.student FROM Teaches gpa JOIN Teaches parent ON gpa.student = parent.advisor JOIN Teaches gc ON parent.student = gc.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS (   SELECT advisor, student   FROM Teaches   UNION   SELECT t.advisor, a.student   FROM Teaches t   JOIN Ancestors a ON t.student = a.advisor )   SELECT * FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (   SELECT id, name, university, year   FROM Scientist   WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'      UNION ALL      SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year   FROM Scientist s   JOIN Teaches t ON s.id = t.student   JOIN Descendants d ON t.advisor = d.id )   SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Answer: SELECT s.name, COUNT(st.id) AS num_students_supervised 2FROM Scientist s 3LEFT JOIN Student st ON s.id = st.supervisor_id 4GROUP BY s.name 5ORDER BY num_students_supervised DESC;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE advisor_hierarchy (advisor, student) AS (  SELECT A1.id, S.id  FROM Advisor A1, Advisor A2, Student S  WHERE A1.id = A2.parent_id  AND A2.id = S.advisor_id  AND A1.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus'  UNION ALL  SELECT AH.advisor, S.id  FROM advisor_hierarchy AH, Advisor A2, Student S  WHERE AH.student = A2.id  AND A2.id = S.advisor_id ) SELECT DISTINCT * FROM advisor_hierarchy;

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT Su.name, count(St.name) AS num_supervised FROM Scientist Su, Scientist St, Teaches T WHERE Su.id = T.advisor AND St.id = T.student GROUP BY Su.name ORDER BY num_supervised DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Su.id, Su.name, Su.university, Su.year   FROM Scientist Su, Scientist St, Teaches T  WHERE St.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND St.id = T.student AND Su.id = T.advisor;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT St.id, St.name, St.university, St.year FROM Scientist Su, Scientist St, Teaches T  WHERE Su.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND Su.id = T.advisor AND St.id = T.student;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT G.id FROM Scientist G, Scientist F, Scientist C, Teaches T, Teaches T2 WHERE G.id = T.advisor AND F.id = T.student AND F.id = T2.advisor AND C.id = T2.student;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Pairs(advisor, student) AS ( SELECT A.id AS advisor, S.id AS student FROM Scientist A, Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE A.id = T.advisor AND S.id = T.student UNION SELECT P.student AS advisor, S.id AS student FROM Pairs P, Scientist S, Teaches T WHERE P.student = T.advisor AND S.id = T.student ) SELECT * FROM Pairs;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE TreeErasmus(id, name, university, year) AS ( SELECT C.id, C.name, C.university, C.year FROM Scientist C, Scientist E, Teaches T WHERE E.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND E.id = T.advisor AND C.id = T.student UNION SELECT C.id, C.name, C.university, C.year FROM Scientist C, TreeErasmus E, Teaches T WHERE E.id = T.advisor AND C.id = T.student AND NOT E.id IS NULL ) SELECT * FROM TreeErasmus;

Answer Match: read1(x); read2(x); write2(x); read1(y); write1(x); write1(y)

Q Text: Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
Answer Match: read1(x); read1(y); write1(x); read2(x); write2(x); write1(y); commit1(x,y); commit2(x)

Q Text: We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1: Serializable and T2 Read Uncommitted

Q Text: Preparation Download the database genealogy2021-10-19.sql, which has the following relational schema:     Scientist(id, name, university, year)    Teaches(advisor, student) In this schema, primary keys are underlined. The attributes advisor and student are foreign keys both referencing id in Scientist. The data was downloaded from the Mathematics Genealogy Project (https://www.genealogy.math.ndsu.nodak.edu/). We will reason about the data as an academic "family" tree where a scientist is a parent of the scientists (the children) they advised and taught during their career. Similarly, we may talk about a scientist's ancestors and descendants. Question Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that defended their PhD thesis at the 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen'  (Please note that the database uses the local, Dutch, spelling).
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university = 'Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen';

Q Text: Write an SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that graduated from any university in Nijmegen, e.g. select universities that contain the string 'Nijmegen'.
Answer: SELECT * FROM Scientist WHERE university LIKE '%Nijmegen%';

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects for each scientist their name, and the number of students they supervised, ordered descending by that count.
Answer: SELECT name, COUNT(student) FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = advisor GROUP BY name ORDER BY COUNT(student) DESC;

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the advisor(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = advisor AND student = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the query that selects (id, name, university and year) of the student(s) of 'Desiderius Erasmus'
Answer: SELECT Scientist.id, Scientist.name, Scientist.university, Scientist.year FROM Scientist, Teaches WHERE id = student AND advisor = (SELECT id FROM Scientist WHERE name = 'Desiderius Erasmus');

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT t1.advisor, t2.student FROM Teaches t1, Teaches t2 WHERE t1.student = t2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Ancestors AS ( SELECT advisor, student FROM Teaches UNION SELECT t.advisor, t.student FROM Teaches t, Ancestors a WHERE t.advisor = a.student AND NOT t.advisor IS NULL )   SELECT advisor, student FROM Ancestors;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE ErasmusDesc AS ( SELECT t.student FROM Teaches t, Scientist s WHERE s.name = 'Desiderius Erasmus' AND t.advisor = s.id UNION SELECT t.student FROM ErasmusDesc E, Teaches t WHERE E.student = t.advisor AND NOT t.student IS NULL ) SELECT DISTINCT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year FROM Scientist s, ErasmusDesc E WHERE s.id = E.student;

Q Text: Give the SQL query that selects (advisor, student) identifiers for which the advisor is the 'grandparent' of the student (so the student is the grandchild of the advisor).
Answer: SELECT DISTINCT T1.advisor, T2.student FROM Teaches T1, Teaches T2 WHERE T1.student=T2.advisor;

Q Text: Give a recursive SQL query that selects pairs of  (advisor, student) identifiers where the advisor is an ancestor of the student (and the student a descendant of the advisor) NB Recursive queries are supported by PostgreSQL, 9.0 or higher, MySQL 8.0 or higher, and SQLite 3.8.3 or higher.
Answer: With Recursive Ancestor As     ( SELECT student, advisor From Teaches  UNION SELECT A.student, T.advisor FROM Ancestor A, Teaches T WHERE A.advisor=T.student) SELECT * FROM Ancestor;

Q Text: Write a (recursive) SQL query that selects (id, name, university, year) of all scientists that are descendants of 'Desiderius Erasmus' (so, Erasmus is an ancestor of each scientist)
Answer: WITH RECURSIVE Descendants AS (  SELECT S2.id, S2.name, S2.university, S2.year  FROM Scientist, Teaches, Scientist AS S2  WHERE Scientist.name='Desiderius Erasmus'  AND Scientist.id=Teaches.advisor  AND S2.id=Teaches.student  UNION   SELECT s.id, s.name, s.university, s.year   FROM Scientist s, Teaches t, Descendants d   where s.id = t.student   and t.advisor = d.id ) SELECT * FROM Descendants;

Q Text: Consider the following 2 transactions T1 and T2, which have read and write statements in a given execution order:     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y).    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x). Each read and write statement corresponds with an SQL SELECT or UPDATE statement, where x and y correspond to data items (in this case books with a certain ISBN number, i.e., the following queries on the table Book.     read1(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    read1(y)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = '0321228383';    write1(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0136067018';    write1(y) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 1' WHERE isbn = '0321228383';     read2(x)  = SELECT title FROM Book WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018';    write2(x) = UPDATE Book SET title = title || ' part 2' WHERE isbn = ' 0136067018'; Question: Give an execution order of the read and write operations that is not serializable.

Given that same transactions T1 and T2 as above, but now we explicitly add a commit statement for each transaction.     T1 : read1(x);  read1(y);  write1(x);  write1(y);  commit1(x,y)    T2 : read2(x);  write2(x);  commit2(x) Question: Give an execution order such that T2 does a dirty read.
We want to prevent dirty reads by transaction T1, and we want to put minimal constraints on the concurrent execution of transactions T1 and T2. What isolation level(s) do we need for T1 and/or T2?
Answer: T1 Read Committed; T2 Read Uncommitted
